paper_title: NESVM: A Fast Gradient Method for Support Vector Machines
core_contribution: NESVM proposes a fast gradient method for SVMs that smooths the non-differentiable hinge loss and ℓ1-norm in the primal SVM formulation, then applies Nesterov's optimal gradient method to achieve O(1/k²) convergence rate. The method combines current and historical gradients to determine descent direction while using Lipschitz constants for step size.
algorithm_steps: Step 1: Smooth the non-differentiable hinge loss using saddle point formulation and prox-function subtraction
Step 2: Calculate gradient of smoothed objective function using ui = median((1-yiXiw)/(μ||Xi||∞), 0, 1)
Step 3: Apply Nesterov's method with two auxiliary optimizations combining current and historical gradients
Step 4: Update solution using weighted combination determined by Lipschitz constant
Step 5: Perform only two matrix-vector multiplications per iteration
key_equations: Eq. 5: Smoothed hinge loss ℓμ = max over u∈Q of ui(1-yiXiw) - μ/2||Xi||∞ui²
Eq. 7: Piecewise smoothed hinge loss approximation with three cases

key_assumptions.assumption: The hinge loss can be adequately approximated by the smoothed version with bounded error
key_assumptions.why_needed: Enables gradient-based optimization on originally non-differentiable objective
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When smooth parameter μ is too large, approximation error becomes significant

key_assumptions.assumption: Lipschitz constant can be computed and remains bounded
key_assumptions.why_needed: Required for determining step size in Nesterov's method
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: With extremely ill-conditioned data matrices or kernel matrices

key_assumptions.assumption: Linear time complexity is achievable through sparse matrix operations
key_assumptions.why_needed: Core efficiency claim of the method
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When feature matrices are completely dense with no exploitable structure

baseline_limitations_identified: SVM-Perf has O(1/√k) convergence rate and struggles with imbalanced classes; Pegasos has O(1/k) rate and poor performance on dense features; traditional solvers have super-linear time complexity
proposed_improvement: Achieves optimal O(1/k²) convergence rate with linear time complexity by smoothing non-differentiable parts and applying Nesterov's method
condition_where_baseline_wins: When features are extremely sparse and dataset size is small, Pegasos might be more efficient due to its simplicity and ability to exploit sparsity
toy_dataset_justification: These datasets provide varying levels of separability and feature density to test the smoothing approach and convergence properties of NESVM
reproducibility_notes: Paper promises MATLAB code availability but implementation details of Nesterov's method adaptation and homotopy continuation require careful attention to convergence criteria

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Controls trade-off between approximation accuracy and smoothness
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Without smoothing (μ→0), method reduces to subgradient with slower convergence

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Accelerates convergence by dynamically decreasing smooth parameter
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Fixed μ would be slower but still maintain O(1/k²) rate

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Core of Nesterov's method for achieving optimal convergence
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would reduce to standard gradient descent with O(1/k) rate

known_failure_modes.scenario: Classes are severely imbalanced or overlapping with large C parameter
known_failure_modes.reason: Large number of support vectors makes constraint finding inefficient
Cannot exploit sparsity as effectively as specialized sparse methods
